perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall pay to Fox, or such other party as Fox may designate in writing, a royalty in the following amount: (a) From the first unit sold, Fox shall earn a royalty at the rate of ***** percent (*****%) of Licensee's Gross Receipts (as defined in the Agreement) from Licensee's sale and distribution of the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall pay to Fox, or such other party as Fox may designate in writing, a royalty in the following amount: (a) Fox shall earn a royalty at the rate of ***** percent (*****%) of Licensee's Gross Receipts (as defined in the Agreement) from Licensee's sale and distribution of the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products. Licensee will process and remit royalty payments to Fox within 90 days of the end of each calendar quarter.
perturbation.explanation: The change extends the payment timeframe to 90 days, which may contradict state laws regarding timely payments, especially if the agreement is interpreted as providing for regular, ongoing royalties that are akin to wage payments.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 204 requires that wages earned during a calendar month be paid within a specified timeframe, typically no later than the regular payday for the next month. While royalty payments may not be strictly classified as wages, if they are regular and ongoing, a 90-day delay could be construed as violating the spirit and intent of the law regarding timely compensation, especially if the agreement operates in California.
perturbation.location: B. IA 2 AUDIO AND VIDEO WIRELESS PRODUCTS ROYALTY: 1.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 204. (a) All wages, other than those mentioned in Section 201, 201.3, 202, 204.1, or 204.2, earned by any person in any employment are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays. Labor performed between the 1st and 15th days, inclusive, of any calendar month shall be paid for between the 16th and the 26th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and labor performed between the 16th and the last day, inclusive, of any calendar month, shall be paid for between the 1st and 10th day of the following month. However, salaries of executive, administrative, and professional employees of employers covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act, as set forth pursuant to Section 13(a)(1) of the Fair Labor Standards Act, as amended through March 1, 1969, in Part 541 of Title 29 of the Code of Federal Regulations, as that pa
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day payment timeframe for royalties, which potentially contradicts California Labor Code § 204. The original text implies immediate payment upon sale, while the changed text allows for a significant delay. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, LAB 204 (a) states that wages earned are due and payable twice during each calendar month, with specific deadlines. While royalties may not strictly be wages, the Law Explanation suggests that regular and ongoing royalties could be interpreted similarly, making a 90-day delay a potential violation. The change thus introduces a potentially illegal delay, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All VGSL and other CSP payments remitted directly to Fox pursuant to the VGSL Agreement and other CSP agreements with respect to the IA Audio and Video Wireless Products shall be treated in the same manner as set forth in Paragraphs 7(b)(ii) and 7(b)(iii) of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All VGSL and other CSP payments remitted directly to Fox pursuant to the VGSL Agreement and other CSP agreements with respect to the IA Audio and Video Wireless Products shall be treated in the same manner as set forth in Paragraphs 7(b)(ii) and 7(b)(iii) of the Agreement. Fox shall have the right to unilaterally modify these terms upon 10 days' notice.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows Fox to unilaterally modify payment terms with only 10 days' notice, which can be considered an insufficient timeframe, contradicting general contract law principles and potentially violating state laws about good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires reasonable notification when an agreement is to be terminated, and this principle extends to material changes to ongoing agreements. Allowing unilateral modification with only 10 days' notice may not be considered reasonable, especially if it significantly impacts the licensee's ability to plan and manage finances.
perturbation.location: B. IA 2 AUDIO AND VIDEO WIRELESS PRODUCTS ROYALTY: 2.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a provision allowing Fox to unilaterally modify payment terms with only 10 days' notice. This contradicts the principle of 'reasonable notification' as highlighted in UCC § 2-309, which states that 'Termination of a contract by one party...requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party'. While this snippet refers specifically to termination, the Law Explanation argues that it extends to material changes to ongoing agreements, suggesting that 10 days' notice may not meet the threshold of 'reasonable notification,' especially if it significantly impacts the licensee's ability to plan and manage finances. The original contract did not have this provision, thus the changed text undermines established legal standards regarding fair notice.

perturbation.original_text: Except as herein expressly amended or by necessary implication modified by this Amendment, the Agreement in all other respects is hereby ratified and shall continue in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as herein expressly amended or by necessary implication modified by this Amendment, the Agreement in all other respects is hereby ratified and shall continue in full force and effect. Any claims arising from this agreement must be brought within 6 months of the event giving rise to the claim.
perturbation.explanation: The modification reduces the statute of limitations for claims to 6 months, potentially conflicting with statutory limitation periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 provides a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. Reducing this period to 6 months is illegal and unenforceable in California.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 6-month statute of limitations for claims, which directly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 337. Snippet 1 explicitly states a four-year statute of limitations for actions upon any contract. The modification reduces the legally prescribed time frame, making the contractual term unenforceable under California law.
